components.role: First gain stage — provides initial amplification and drives Q2 into saturation
Second gain stage — heavily saturated, produces the main fuzz clipping
Q1 collector resistor — sets first stage gain and headroom
Q1 emitter resistor — stabilizes bias, small value for high gain
Q1 base bias resistor — sets Q1 operating point
Q2 collector resistor — sets output level and final clipping behavior
Q2 base bias — fed from Q1 collector, sets second stage drive
Fuzz control — varies amount of signal fed from Q2 collector back to Q1, controls sustain/fuzz intensity
Input coupling — blocks DC, low value reduces bass for tighter fuzz
Q1 emitter bypass — increases high-frequency gain of first stage

dsp_model.description: Very high gain cascaded stages into hard clipping with lowpass filtering from BJT bandwidth

behavioral_description: A classic two-transistor silicon fuzz producing thick, aggressive, sustaining distortion. Both transistors are driven deep into saturation, creating a heavily clipped, nearly square wave output. The sound is buzzy, thick, and harmonically dense with strong odd-order harmonics. The fuzz control pot adjusts feedback between stages, varying from gated sputtery fuzz at low settings to full, thick sustaining fuzz at maximum. Silicon transistors produce a brighter, more aggressive fuzz than germanium equivalents. The inherent bandwidth limiting of the BJTs rolls off the extreme high frequencies, preventing ice-pick harshness. The input coupling cap is intentionally small to roll off bass before clipping, preventing muddy, flubby fuzz tones. This topology is the basis of the Fuzz Face (silicon version) and Electro-Harmonix Big Muff variations.
